data.title: KULR Technology: Shareholder Dilution Continues Unabatedly - Sell
data.description:
KULR Technology Overview

Note:

I have covered KULR Technology Group, Inc. (NYSE:KULR) or “KULR Technology” previously, so investors should view this as an update to my earlier articles on the company.

After the close of Thursday's regular session, KULR Technology reported second quarter results well above consensus expectations:

Revenues of $4.0 million represented a new all-time high for the company while GAAP earnings per share of $0.22 also coming in well above the ($0.12) consensus estimate.

However, adjusted for contributions related to KULR Technology's recently initiated Bitcoin treasury strategy, revenues were up just slightly on a year-over-year basis while the company's loss from operations increased by almost 80% to $9.5 million.

Record cash burn of $12.1 million wasn't suited to instill confidence in the core business, either.

During the quarter, KULR Technology raised an additional $38.3 million from selling approximately 3.8 million newly-issued shares into the open market. Subsequent to quarter-end, the company sold an additional 1.6 million shares into the open market for gross proceeds of $10.7 million.

Please note that KULR Technology conducted an 1:8 reverse stock split on June 20.

Most of the proceeds were used to purchase additional Bitcoin. As of the end of the second quarter, the company held 928.28 Bitcoin with a market value of $99.5 million. Subsequent to quarter-end, KULR Technology increased its total Bitcoin holdings to 1,035 with an aggregate market value of $121.3 million.

The company has also entered into a number of Bitcoin mining services agreements, as outlined in more detail in its quarterly report on form 10-Q:

On March 7, 2025, the Company entered into a sixty-day lease agreement (the “First Machine Lease Agreement”) with a digital asset mining services company to operate digital assets mining machines on KULR’s behalf, at a total lease cost of $850,000.

Additionally, on May 16, 2025, the Company entered into a two hundred and twenty eight-day lease agreement (the “Second Machine Lease Agreement”) with the same digital asset mining services company to operate digital assets mining machines on KULR’s behalf, at a total lease cost of $3,200,000.

Furthermore, on June 20, 2025, the Company entered into a one hundred and three-day lease agreement (the “Third Machine Lease Agreement”) with a new digital asset mining services company to operate digital assets mining machines on KULR’s behalf, at a total lease cost of $2,756,795. During the three and six months ended June 30, 2025, the Company recognized revenue of $1,439,095 and $1,688,849, respectively, in connection with its digital assets mining operations.

While only a limited number of Bitcoin (BTC-USD) will be mined under these agreements, they enable the company to recognize mining revenues. Due to the additional lease agreements signed in the second quarter, I would expect Bitcoin mining revenues to increase to approximately $3.6 million in the third quarter thus covering more than 70% of the current consensus estimate.

During the questions-and-answers session of the conference call, a number of investors complained hard about the persistent lack of operational progress, broken promises, aggressive dilution and the disastrous share price performance. Quite frankly, I can't blame them:

Following the most recent stock price decline, KULR Technology's valuation has declined to more reasonable levels:

However given the lack of operational progress and substantially negative free cash flow, I am struggling to assign any sort of meaningful value to the tiny core business at this point.

In addition, the company just entered into a massive $300 million open market sales agreement with Cantor Fitzgerald & Co. and Craig-Hallum Capital Group, LLC. As a result, I would expect shareholder dilution to continue unabatedly for the time being.

At this point, KULR Technology remains almost solely a bet on the Bitcoin price appreciating even further. But even with Bitcoin trading near all-time highs, the company's stock price has continued to decline due to persistent open market sales which are not likely to stop anytime soon.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on KULR Technology's common shares.

Risk Factors

As usual, the bear thesis is not without risk. The most obvious catalysts for the stock are:

  • termination of the company's open market sales agreement.
  • further Bitcoin price appreciation.
  • substantial improvements in the company's operating performance.

Particularly the removal of the open market sales agreement overhang would likely attract a new round of speculative investors and traders to the stock.

Bottom Line

KULR Technology reported second quarter earnings well ahead of expectations. However, record revenues and profitability were solely the result of the company's recently initiated Bitcoin treasury strategy. In fact, operating results showed record losses and persistent cash burn.

Given the lack of progress in the core business and ongoing overhang from aggressive open market sales, I am reiterating my "Sell" rating on KULR Technology's common shares.

data.title: IVES: Too Big Tech Focused
data.description:
For investors unfamiliar with Dan Ives, he is always easy to spot on CNBC or other media outlets as he’s often wearing outlandish and eye-catching clothing. Despite Ives' questionable attire selection he is one of the most prominent tech analysts today with more than two decades worth of experience researching the tech sector. Ives has been correct on numerous bullish tech calls including, Palantir (PLTR) and Tesla (TSLA)

Earlier this summer, Ives and the team at Wedbush launched the Dan Ives Wedbush AI Revolution ETF (NYSEARCA:IVES). Let’s dive into the fund’s strategy and current top holdings as I discuss why this ETF may be well-suited for some growth investors, especially those looking to get some AI exposure.

Fund Strategy and Holdings

The ETF’s strategy is simple, find the companies that are poised to lead the artificial intelligence revolution. Ives believes tech investing is undergoing a “golden age” with the emergence of AI and that will generate huge returns for investors.

When discussing his ETF Ives stated, “I believe the market is still massively underestimating what the growth is going to look like for the AI revolution in tech. For us, it’s not just Mag 7. It’s not just those first four or five names. It’s trying to identify names that maybe today thematically you don’t even consider an AI name.”

The fund currently has 30 stocks including many Mag 7 and chip stocks known to tech investors:

Looking at the top holdings in IVES, I can quickly see that this fund is heavy in the Mag 7 and I’d argue the first twelve holdings are large, well-known companies. Looking down the list further, you’ll see more familiar names such as Salesforce (CRM), IBM, and Adobe (ADBE). To me, the theme of this ETF is that big tech will continue to lead the AI boom as these firms have the capital to continue to spend.

This chart from the Wall Street Journal also clearly illustrates tech giants, Meta, Google, Microsoft, and Amazon are splurging on AI capital expenditures:

Many investors have likely seen the news of Meta trying to poach AI talent by offering them ridiculous salaries, all in the hopes of winning the big tech AI arms race. Clearly, big tech is spending, spending, spending, in hopes of dominating the AI revolution.

Now, there are some issues I have with the fund being so heavy into large, established tech giants.

First, let's discuss if this spending will result in AI dominance. In a blog post, popular finance writer, Ben Carlson noted two probable outcomes associated with AI spending. One outcome is that these tech giants dominate because obviously they spent so much money on this world-changing technology. The second outcome is that these companies crash and burn because the ROI was less than predicted and expectations were far too high. Obviously, it doesn't have to be one or the other either, but I think Carlson does bring up a good point that spending doesn't ensure future success. One need look no further than Zuckerberg's metaverse spending.

Next, I think it’s a miss this fund isn’t including many stocks associated with the infrastructure necessary for these tech giants to continue to power their data centers and their overall AI efforts. The ETF does include Oklo, Inc. (OKLO) which I’d say is still a speculation stock. Whereas, Tom Lee’s Granny Shot ETF (GRNY) has more solid energy and infrastructure plays such as GE Vernova (GEV), Quanta Services (PWR), Eaton (ETN) and Emerson Electric (EMR). To me, GRNY is in a great position to not only benefit from AI by holding the key tech players but they are also in a position to benefit from the power these tech giants will need.

I also think IVES misses on a few big tech themes that some may argue aren’t pure AI plays but I think are still associated with IT such as cryptocurrency and advancements in healthcare.

Looking at the holdings of IVES, there is nothing that looks like a proxy crypto play or in general a firm that will benefit from the rise of cryptocurrency. GRNY on the other hand has Robinhood (HOOD), Paypal (PYPL) and Strategy (MSTR) which is certainly a Bitcoin play. ARKK meanwhile also has Robinhood as a top ten holding as well as Coinbase. I’d argue this is a big miss for the fund.

In my opinion, it’s harder to marry AI and healthcare but GRNY has at least attempted to speculate on which companies might continue to do well with selections of Abbott Laboratories (ABT) and Intuitive Surgical (ISRG). While these aren’t particularly risky bets, I do think Intuitive Surgical is a good bet in terms of healthcare companies that can successfully integrate AI and robotics. ARKK on the other hand has far more bets on healthcare with CRISPR Therapeutics and Tempus AI in the fund’s top holdings.

While there is no breakdown on Seeking Alpha of IVES I think it’s safe to say these companies all fall into the tech category.

GRNY currently has 40 holdings and as you can see in the graphic below, the holdings are primarily technology but financials and industrials also make up a good chunk of the ETF:

Of the three different ETFs, ARKK is most diversified with healthcare as the highest category:

To quickly summarize, IVES I believe is all tech with a strong belief the industry giants will keep on leading the AI charge. GRNY is primarily tech focused but financials and industrials also make up a large portion of the fund. I’d say ARKK does have more of a “shotgun” approach with their strategy as this fund has the more diverse holdings.

Fees and Risks

When it comes to ETFs as a long term investor, I’m focused on two key aspects: minimizing fees and reducing my risk.

As it relates to fees, IVES has an expense ratio of 0.75% which is higher than the sector median as you can see below:

This expense ratio is the same as both GRNY and ARKK.

While there is limited risk information on IVES, you can see from the graphic above the weight of the holdings is rather spread out with no one significant holding in the top ten.

GRNY also has only roughly 33% of the fund allocated to the top ten holdings which is less than median for all ETFs as you can see below:

ARKK on the other hand has 57% of their fund allocated to their top ten holdings as you can see below as they have a significant bet on Tesla:

As shown above ARKK is particularly risky ETF as the fund’s overall risk grade is a F. ARKK is clearly very volatile with an annualized volatility far exceeding the median for all ETFs. I also don’t like that ARKK’s turnover rate is higher than the median. I’ve discussed this in a prior article, but outside of a few holdings such as Tesla, Wood seems to lack conviction in many of her stock picks which is a huge negative in my opinion.

In comparison, GRNY has a turnover rate of 32% which is above the median for all ETFs but is much lower than ARKK’s 39%. IVES doesn’t have this information available yet. From watching IVES on CNBC he appears rather consistent in his picks, frequently talking bullishly about companies such as Nvidia (NVDA) and Palantir (PLTR). However, actions speak louder than words so I’ll be closely watching the turnover percentage for this ETF moving forward.

Now, tech can be very volatile especially when the market rotates out of the sector but the Mag 7 have really driven the market over the last few years and I don’t see AI flaming out like the metaverse did. Considering IVES is full of tech giants with the capital to spend on AI and this fund has relatively equal weight amongst its top holdings I don’t think it’s a particularly risky ETF, especially compared to ARKK.

Despite the fact that IVES is what I would consider the least risky of the three ETFs I’ve mentioned thus far, if I were going to pay the higher expenses for one of these funds I’d go with GRNY. I think Tom Lee and his team have selected a more diverse group of holdings that cover more themes than just artificial intelligence. With GRNY, investors are getting access to many of the Mag 7 stocks as well as other investing themes like industrial stocks (associated with energy and global labor supply themes) and financial stocks (associated with generational wealth transfer and cryptocurrency).

Alternatively, as I’m a fan of lower fees, investors wanting to get tech exposure could invest in Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (QQQ) which has an expense ratio of only 0.20% and is less risky compared to the funds I’ve previously mentioned, as QQQ only has a turnover rate of roughly 8%.

Also, with over 100 holdings, I think QQQ has a better chance of finding a winning mid-cap or small-cap stock outside of the Magnificent 7 and is considerably more diversified compared to IVES as you can see below:

Conclusion

I think Dan Ives is a brilliant analyst and he certainly knows the tech industry. I also agree with his assessment that a good number of the tech giants of today will continue to dominate as AI begins to change our world.

However, with such a large allocation of his fund going to tech giants I’m questioning whether or not these leaders can continue to generate outsized gains.

I also think IVES misses the mark with some key exclusions to the fund especially around Bitcoin and cryptocurrency.

I don’t like high fees when I’m allocating my capital nor do I like funds that are particularly risky. IVES isn’t as risky as ARKK but considering it’s all tech I’d be more inclined to buy QQQ as it has a known history, is less risky, is more diversified than IVES and is less expensive. If I were going to allocate any of my capital to one of three funds with higher fees, I’d go with Tom Lee’s GRNY.

data.title: Innovative Industrial Properties: Life Science Pivot, Rescheduling Progress, And Tenant Changes
data.description:
Innovative Industrial Properties (NYSE:IIPR) has faced intense earnings pressure from tenant rent defaults that now mean the REIT is unable to cover its dividend to common shareholders. This forms a reason to be more neutral on the commons even as the industry faces a possible step change from the Trump administration floating an executive action to reclassify cannabis as a less dangerous drug, possibly before the end of summer. A shift of cannabis from Schedule I, the most prohibitive schedule, to Schedule III would not legalize cannabis on the federal level but would reduce the tax burden, unlock greater access to capital, and open up to sector to greater institutional participation. Fundamentally, the move would boost access to capital to an industry that's been starved of liquidity, alleviating credit stress for multi-state operators ("MSOs") and driving greater outcomes for IIPR. I last covered IIPR in May.

IIPR last declared a quarterly cash dividend of $1.90 per share, kept unchanged from its prior distribution, and $7.60 per share annualized for a 14.30% dividend yield. IIPR once traded at a dividend yield as low as 2.2% in 2021. The REIT's fortress balance sheet, dividend hikes, and strong FFO growth then provided reasons for extreme bullishness and the commons traded as high as $275 per share. The 9.00% Series A Preferreds (NYSE:IIPR.PR.A) also traded at an extreme 48% premium to their $25 per share liquidation value, but this has been compressed to a small 0.84% discount. Is the current dividend yield a buy? Not entirely. The coverage is negative as IIPR works through tenant defaults.

Dividend Coverage And Liquidity

IIPR generated fiscal 2025 second-quarter revenue of $62.89 million, down 21.2% over its year-ago comp, a sequential dip of around 12%, and a miss by $960,000 on consensus estimates. The figure wasn't great, with the drop led by tenant defaults that continue to compound angst. The REIT realized funds from operations ("FFO") of $1.56 per share, also missing consensus estimates and dropping from FFO of $2.06 per share a year ago. Critically, the FFO miss means IIPR is only currently covering its dividend by 82%, a roughly 122% payout ratio. IIPR currently spends $54.4 million every quarter in aggregate payments to its common shareholders, there is another $672,000 in payments to its preferred shareholders. This is set against free cash flow of $41 million generated for its second quarter. IIPR held cash and cash equivalents of $99.67 million as of the end of the second quarter, a drop of 17.5% over its year-ago comp but at a level where the company can meet the current dividend coverage gap.

To be clear, assuming IIPR is unable to work through the current tenant defaults and there are no further defaults, the REIT would be able to maintain the current dividend to shareholders for seven quarters, just under two years. This would not be prudent but IIPR has the flexibility due to just how lowly geared its balance sheet is. Gross debt as of the end of the second quarter was $291.2 million, a roughly 11% debt to gross assets ratio and a 0.15x debt to equity ratio. The REIT bought back $20.1 million worth of its common shares in the six months since the start of 2025, but is unlikely to continue this following the ICHQ transaction.

Tenant Defaults And Life Science Pivot

PharmaCann's defaults on its leases with IIPR have formed the core headwind for the REIT. The MSO would have been IIPR's top tenant by rent as of the end of the second quarter, driving 11.5% of annualized base rent ("ABR"). PharmaCann's non-payment of rent represents a significant but temporary headwind, with IIPR now focused on pursuing eviction to recover its properties for re-lease to qualified operators. It's unlikely the REIT will be able to recover the unpaid rent. What would be interesting is what impact cannabis rescheduling would have on IIPR's ability to re-let these spaces or if it prompts PharmaCann to make good on its obligations. IIPR did not provide guidance on timing for the recovery of these properties and management should look to spell these out for their third-quarter earnings. Their current debt is coming due next year on May 25.

IIPR also made its first non-cannabis investment, leading a $270 million investment in IQHQ, a private life science REIT with properties in San Diego, Boston, San Francisco, and the UK. The investment will immediately be accretive to AFFO with a blended yield of 14%, consisting of a $100 million investment in IQHQ's revolving credit facility and $170 million in IQHQ's preferred stock. The move can either be positive or negative from a dividend perspective. On the positive side, the returns from the ICHQ investment and its inherent diversification could build more resilience for coverage. On the negative side, the requirement for IIPR to fund the investment could hasten the pace and extent of a possible dividend cut. IIPR does have more upside potential from its current level on a possible move by the administration to reschedule cannabis and as the REIT works through its tenant defaults. I'm rating the commons as a hold.

data.title: AMD: Reiterating Strong Buy, Don't Count It Out But Expectations Are High
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:AMD) Q2 earnings slightly disappointed investors as the stock fell slightly directly post earnings, especially since the stock had built large momentum and optimism pre-earnings. Despite the dip, AMD has already recovered from the pullback as optimism around the reasons why it dipped has been clarified.

Since my last thesis, AMD has outperformed the S&P 500 (SP500) by a good margin despite the drop post earnings. The main idea was that inference would be the majority of the total addressable market, or TAM, compared to training in the future (predicted by AMD in the AI advancing keynote). Hence, AMD is positioning itself to not compete with Nvidia with training now but rather having processing power like memory bandwidth and storage to compete in inference. With the MI400X coming out in 2026 and the export license headwind cleared up, there is huge optimism for AMD's future growth roadmap.

AMD CEO Lisa Su has already doing well in the CPU space against Intel, hitting new highs and closing the unit-share gap (explained later), now positioning AMD for high growth in GPUs through inference. Nvidia's moat will most likely still be wide, but the growth potential of AMD remains high with only a small chunk of the data center market share. Hence, I'm reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

Nvidia and AMD reenter China

Nvidia and AMD have agreed with President Trump to hand over 15% of their China revenue to reopen their China markets as a fee. There is no real direct comparison of such an agreement happening ever, but the 15% will affect AMD's margins. AMD has lower margins than Nvidia, so from this perspective, AMD is being heavily affected as Nvidia can work with its affected margins better than AMD.

However, I believe that despite the 15%, there is still much more positive sentiment around this deal. It was noted in their press release that

second quarter results were impacted by the U.S. Government's export control on our AMD Instinct™ MI308 data center GPU products. For the quarter, these restrictions led to approximately $800 million in inventory and related charges,

This would clear up inventory that already exists, as we remember that these are the older model processors AMD is getting rid of. This could result in additional revenue of ~1 billion as soon as Q3 or could be processed later during Q4.

In 2024, AMD's China revenue constituted 24% of their total revenue at $6.2billion. This is a large portion of AMD's revenue. There have been deals in the last few months, such as the deal with the Middle East, to cover the lost revenue of China, but now that will be considered additional revenue.

Since this is the first time that such an agreement is in place, for me there are also questions about what they are actually doing with the received revenue. Security concerns have been a huge concern for the U.S. to allow the most advanced chips to be exported to China, as this 15% could contribute to ensuring the security of the best chips in the world to arrive at their final destination (smuggling of top chips into China). The silver lining for me is that if the 15% fee is used towards the AI industry anyway, the 15% fee in the bigger picture wouldn't be a huge issue. This could go towards the Chips Act, which Trump indicated he wanted to cancel months ago, or any positives towards the industry itself.

The result of AMD being able to access China again obviously benefits AMD more than it will negatively affect it. The ability to sell off old inventory and reopen a large market for AMD only has positive implications. However, Nvidia's margins will be better than AMD's due to its scale and higher gross margins, which could create a larger gap in the short term. Nvidia did $17.1billion in China business last fiscal year, which stood at 13.1% of their total sales. Their sales to China are a smaller portion of their revenue compared to AMD, which will impact Nvidia less than it will AMD. To me, this isn't a big deal, as investors are buying AMD because of their future growth potential in their MI400X, as Citi also points out.

It is widely speculated (and probably true) that the most advanced AI in the U.S. is being used as a bargaining tool rather than the main reason being "security concerns." There are obviously security concerns for both sides, but the long-term vision remains as AMD can fully access China's market with no tariffs or any regulations. Tensions are already easing up, as the next quarter will show demand for MI308X in China, and hopefully, much more positive sentiment in this deal. In the most recent quarter, the guided earnings did not include MI308X revenue, as the export license hasn't been fully approved, which is what I believed led the stock downwards as investors wanted clarity on guidance with the MI308X. Guidance with MI308X is still unclear, but I would like to see at least the current inventory of ~$800M. A bullish stance would be more than $1.5B, as this is the amount AMD has estimated its revenue will be hit by U.S. curbs on Chinese exports.

Optimistic for MI400X with MI350X price hikes

I did a product review before on AMD. My main takeaway was in terms of processing speed and specs for inference: AMD's was better than Nvidia's. However, Nvidia's CUDA is the main reason that AMD falls behind, as with their closed-source ecosystem, Nvidia is able to dominate the GPU space. CUDA is way more mature than AMD's ROCm, as it is easier to deploy, higher performance and streamlined for clients. The whole ecosystem matters, both hardware and software, as Nvidia dominates when both hardware and software are considered, which leads to a majority market share in AI accelerator revenue for Nvidia.

CUDA's downside is being closed source, as AMD's open-source ROCm could be better suited for certain clients to deploy and optimize to better suit their needs. ROCm 7 also has massive improvements compared to ROCm 6, with an average of 3.5x performance improvement in terms of inference and a 3x performance improvement in terms of training. CUDA is better than ROCm, but continuous improvement and being open-sourced don't fully count out AMD.

Nvidia is also better at training than AMD, as the TAM for training is larger right now, but AMD is positioning itself to be better in inference.

Some reports also showed plans that AMD was raising the prices of its MI350X by 70%. It will still be cheaper than Blackwell if it happens, but this to me shows confidence in their product and overall demand. This has clarified to me that my product review is not far off Nvidia's offerings, and it can compete with Nvidia despite Nvidia dominating the data center market, as demand for MI350X is strong.

Their recent earnings call assured that MI400X is on track.

MI400 series development is progressing well towards our planned launch in 2026, with significant interest in large-scale deployments from multiple high-profile customers,

Their data center revenue is positioned for high growth alongside its recovered China revenue. With major clients such as OpenAI already involved in MI400X, as Sam Altman took the stage in their AI advancing keynote event, such interest is are major catalyst for MI400X's demand and optimism towards their new line of products. Other major clients such as Meta will likely be involved in providing feedback like OpenAI, as I expect MI400X's demand to be through the roof.

In Q2 earnings, AMD only reported a 14% YoY increase in data center revenue which excluded MI308X. In Q3, data center revenue growth has to impress investors as the valuation is getting higher and higher as momentum continues to build. There should hopefully be more clarification with their China GPU revenue in the near term, and more clarification with their MI400X demand in Q3, which are all catalysts for AMD to further grow.

My thesis is still on the fact that AMD's GPU isn't that far behind Nvidia's except for software (CUDA is superior to ROCm), but their market caps have a huge difference. Optimism for AMD continues to build but it needs to show actual numbers for the gap to close. If AMD even captures 10% of the data center market in 2028, of which they currently capture 4% (being bullish), their GPU revenue alone would be ~$50 billion. Considering AMD is also consistently taking CPU market share from Intel, a market cap of only ~$285 billion right now isn't that expensive.

Further optimism

AMD continues to outperform Intel in the CPU market. AMD has been slowly taking Intel's annual CPU market share, from its low point of 11% in 2014 to 24.7% in 2025 so far, with estimates that AMD will eventually get to ~39.2% in 2029. The most recent Intel earnings call further supports this, as management mentions referring to servers and CPUs:

As far as share goes, obviously, we're not where we want to be in terms of a competitive portfolio, and that's what Lip-Bu is really focused on improving.

To me, this is Intel admitting that their level of CPU is not close to AMD's, and is still significantly behind AMD's.

In terms of unit share in Q2, AMD fell by 0.2% from 24.4% to 24.2% unit share in the CPU market. However, their revenue share increased from 31.7% to 33%. Remember, AMD supplies the best high-end CPUs in the industry. YoY their unit share increased from 21.3% to 24.4%, whilst revenue share increased from 24.2% to 33%, showing that AMD unit share and revenue in CPUs are all trending upwards. If their unit share increases to ~40% as estimates have in 2029, they would have over 50% of revenue share in the CPU market, which is in my view very achievable with their product roadmap in Venice and Verano. In the same source, it was also said that "Intel now outsells AMD 2:1, down from 8:2 in 2023." This means that either Intel CPU shares have halved or AMD has doubled (most likely a bit of both), which further shows optimism for AMD's CPU market and its long-term trajectory.

Another optimistic news recently is that TSMC (TSM) is very likely to be exempt from the 100% chip tariffs that were proposed by Trump due to its investments in the U.S. This only has positive implications for AMD being a fabless firm. This clears out the macroeconomic headwinds that TSMC faces with Trump's tariffs, as TSMC can have more stability in planning and maintaining its operations. With six more planned fabs to be built in the U.S., AMD is a beneficiary of this with no additional cost and a simpler supply chain (the semiconductor supply chain is still very complex).

Risks

AMD needs to show numbers and major clients for their new MI400X, as their data center revenue growth is still low. As mentioned before, their Q2 earnings only showed a 14% YoY growth in data center revenue, which means that investors are buying into the growth potential of their data center revenue through MI400X.

Due to such optimism and investors buying into AMD, as it keeps building momentum, it leads to a high P/E Non-GAAP relative to its sector. On a forward P/E non-GAAP basis, Nvidia only trades at ∼42, which is an indicator that AMD, on certain metrics trades at a higher multiple than giant Nvidia. If the growth potential and demand disappoint over the next few quarters or any form of negative sentiment arises around their MI400X, AMD's high valuations won't be justified.

The thesis is also buying into the fact that AMD can capture market share from Nvidia, as AMD's hardware is better for inference than Nvidia's, as the TAM for AI accelerators is expected to be positioned more heavily towards inference rather than training(graph above from AI advancing keynote). If Nvidia remains the top pick for clients despite AMD's hardware being better suited for inference, AMD's growth potential will fall as they will only be able to appeal to clients through cheaper lower model AI accelerators, rather than having the best AI accelerator which top hyperscalers demand and pay billions for.

Takeaways and my expectations

AMD's product roadmap is going well, with its MI400X out in 2026. Their GPU gap with Nvidia in my view is now in their software, as AMD's hardware in terms of storage, bandwidth and memory bests over Nvidia's Blackwell. Their CPU market trajectory is also, as expected, all trending upwards, and I don't expect it to slow down. The increase in price to me already shows the strength of their current line of GPUs, which makes me even more optimistic for MI400X in 2026.

With China revenue starting to get clearer, I expect higher guidance for the year in Q3 earnings by hopefully at least $1 billion due to their spare inventory and hopefully robust demand. AMD cannot be late with their MI400X, as I believe it is the largest catalyst and growth factor for AMD to increase in market cap.

Data center revenue growth also has to impress in the next few quarters, as Q2 only had low teens growth. This is nowhere close to what Nvidia is growing at in terms of number and percentage. Margins should improve next quarter with the 70% price hike, but I would like to see a higher growth % number than just low teens. I also would like to see more major customers/hyperscalers come out and adopt the MI400X series, as this would ensure the shift that inference will be larger than training in the future, and AMD is gaining market share because of it.

Expectations are high, as investors are buying into the growth story of AMD, with the stock pricing more than doubling since its April lows and achieving a 52-week high recently. In my opinion, if any of the above metrics disappoint investors, the stock price will fall(assuming all other metrics don't absolutely exceed expectations). Guidance for the rest of 2025 and more clarification are key catalysts and need to impress.

Conclusion

I still hold a large position in AMD and did slightly increase the position directly post earnings, as I believe that there will be much more optimism coming out in the near future. AMD still only holds around 4% of the GPU market as mentioned before, which perfectly positions AMD to increase its market share with its MI400X. The whole ecosystem is important, which is why Nvidia is more favorable with CUDA, but the better hardware and prices in my opinion perfectly position AMD to take market share.

2026 will be an exciting year for AMD with their new CPUs and GPUs, as if they start taking market share which I believe they will heavily in GPUs with OpenAI's endorsement already, could lead to high growth that will close the huge gap with Nvidia. It doesn't need to completely close the gap, but from a 4% market share and an underdog position, I believe AMD is perfectly positioned for high growth with their new GPUs, as their CPUs are already consistently taking market share and will continue to do so. Therefore, my rating is maintained at a Strong Buy with huge optimism for AMD.

data.title: Astera's Quiet Push Into AI Racks
data.description:
Astera Labs (NASDAQ:ALAB) is currently valued like a “parts supplier” but operates more like a platform company, shaping how data moves across the entire AI rack. Astera’s open-standards fabric and fleet software could prove as sticky as proprietary networks, helping it to capture more dollar content per accelerator while keeping hyperscalers comfortable, sourcing from multiple vendors. Recent execution suggests that it’s already happening.

The Silent Fabric Land-Grabbing

Astera's Q2 numbers did more than beat, as they reestablished the baseline. Revenue hit $191.9 million, up 20% quarter over quarter and 150 % year over year, with non-GAAP gross margin at 76% and operating cash flow at $135.4 million. Q3 guidance came in at $203-$210 million with gross margin in the 75% range, implying flat volumes and mix, even with hardware module growth continuing. The balance sheet now sports around $1.07 billion in cash and securities, providing Astera with a self-funded runway to ride into the next product wave.

Beneath the headline numbers, the underlying shifts are telling. Scorpio P‑Series PCIe 6 fabric switch­es moved into volume manufacturing and crossed the 10% revenue threshold during their first ramp quarter, and Aries 6 retimers began volume shipments in merchant GPU racks. At the same time, Taurus active optical cable expanded with 800G Ethernet adoption, with adoption momentum beginning to build.

The company also announced additional relationships with Nvidia through NVLink Fusion, a collabora­tion with high-performance ASIC shop Alchip, and key sponsorship of UALink as the only open memory-semantic scale-up fabric. These three broaden Astera's applicability around scale-up and scale-out architectures without locking customers into one sole proprie­­tary stack.

Margins With Muscle: How Astera Balances Hyper-Growth and Discipline

Beyond the top line, the income statement shows operating leverage unusually clean for a hyper-growth semiconductor company. Non-GAAP operating margin came in at 39.2% in Q2 from 33.7 % in Q1 as product mix was largely flat even as volumes improved. Noisy at the tax line due to a change in law mid-year, guidance sensibly reverts to a 15% full-year non-GAAP rate. The net effect is self-disciplined profitability as Astera continues to add people for R&D and expand its footprint.

The 10‑Q sheds valuable color on margin dynamics and mix. The gross margin is lower by 210 basis points year over year because the company shipped a higher percentage of hardware modules. That's not a quality issue, but it's a predictable result of entering high‑volume AECs and system hardware where gross margins are inherently lower than stand‑alone silicon. That mix shift causes higher dollar content per rack and accelerator. It also builds a moat as after customers integrate cables, retimers, and fabric under a unified software layer, switching suppliers carries meaningful operational risk.

Another story is cash conversion. In the first half of 2025, the operating cash flow of $145.9 million implies earnings quality and working capital discipline under 147% year-on-year revenue growth. Inventory had been accumulated before expected demand, but accounts receivable turned out in our favor, and Astera deployed excess cash into marketable securities. From the balance sheet, $1.18 billion of current assets and only $106 million of current liabilities show strategic flexibility for equipment investment, hiring, and future M&A.

Two caveats are in order. First, stock-based compensation is still significant at $35.5 million in Q2 and $77.9 million for the year. That’s typical for newly public, high-growth silicon ventures, but non-GAAP reporting is justified in leaving it out. Still, investors need to model future dilution as they do with the reported non-GAAP measures from the company. Reconciliation tables clearly show the impact, with non-GAAP operating income of $75.2 million vs. $39.8 million GAAP operating income in Q2.

Second, revenue has a contra-revenue item for customer warrants, reducing recognized revenue by $1.8 million in Q2. The remaining warrant value will continue to get recognized as an offset proportionally until 2029, associated with sales. Neither item impairs earnings quality, but both affect the accuracy of forecasting.

The supply side appears diversified by product but not by customers or geography. During Q2, there were three customers with 27%, 24% and 13% of revenue respectively, two others with 12% and 11%. Geographically, Singapore, China, and Taiwan have captured the majority of shipments with the United States being a single‑digit contributor by billing address this quarter. Concentration is typical in this stage because hyperscalers and a few tier‑one OEMs control deployments in AI rack, but it heightens forecasting volatility and trade restriction exposure.

Last but not least, the other benefit is the tax position of the company aligns with July's federal law as part of the One Big Beautiful Act, which repeals mandatory capitalization of US R&D. This, as advocated for by Astera's intention, allows for the immediate expensing of R&D along with ongoing 100% bonus depreciation that would be allowed for qualifying properties. Astera maintains that implications are considered, but the trend would have the tendency to support GAAP margins and reduce cash taxes by making substantial investment in the development of a new product.

ALAB’s Premium Price Tag Signals a Bet on Unstoppable Growth

With a $194 stock price, Astera Labs carries a ~$32 billion market cap and ~$31 billion enterprise value after $1.07 billion in cash. That's forward multiples of ~121x P/E, and ~40x EV/Sales, well below sector medians. These kinds of premiums earn poor grades on traditional relative screens. Still, the market is putting Astera stock in the category of a few AI rack-scale infrastructure platforms with software-like economics.

Revenue for ALAB is poised to increase and reach $1.02 billion in 2026, rising 31%. Even at the bottom of the estimate range, growth is robust with estimates closely bunched together, indicating analyst optimism and probable clarity from locked-in design wins. Forward P/S ratios compress from 41.4x to 31.7x strictly on revenue growth and not rerating, so the premium the stock sells for today becomes less scary.

This multiple compression combined with extreme faith in top-line growth implies ALAB is fast growing into its valuation. Revenue beats on the figures can boost the re-rating, rendering expensive-looking stock a steal in retrospect.

Finally, a PEG of ~2.1, on the ~60% earnings growth consensus implies, is respectable for a segment leader in the midst of the segment's hypergrowth phase. In the chip world, where segment leaders often have PEGs above 3 in the same phase, this puts ALAB in the GARP camp instead of where the market story suggests.

Key Risks

Astera Labs faces risks of cyclicality and concentration. Five Q2 customers accounted for the vast majority of revenue, with top Singapore, China, and Taiwan exposure, sectors subject to geopolitical and trade disruption. Deficiencies in internal controls of risk assessment, segregation of duties, and IT controls persist under remediation and pose risks to reporting.

Gross margins could compress as lesser-margin modules and switch sales overtake high-margin silicon sales, especially atc ompetitive pricing levels. Hyperscaler AI CapEx has project-like characteristics and can fluctuate abruptly with architectural changes. Competition in proprietary interconnect standards can consume share, and stock-based compensation (~$35.5 million in Q2) can dilute stock if growth slows down. Cash on the balance sheet is $1.07 billion and is comforting, but ongoing innovation and operational discipline are still required.

Conclusion

Astera Labs has the rare combination of hypergrowth and disciplined execution, and PEG and P/S compression signal quicker growth into valuation versus peers. Acceleration of the ecosystem and execution of design win can justify premium multiples, but mix shift, customer concentration, and geopolitical risks are to be watched closely.

data.title: Accenture: The Slump Has Gone Way Too Far
data.description:
After major stock declines due to DOGE headwinds, I initiated Accenture plc (NYSE:ACN) at a buy rating. I saw the drop as an overreaction as the company provided a resilient outlook despite an unfavorable environment. However, after a modest bounce in the stock, declines have resumed and now losses stand at around 13% since the publishing of my article. In the below analysis, it is shown that the fundamentals of the company continue to be strong. In addition, full year guidance was raised for many metrics while the valuation has continued to contract. Therefore, I believe the risk/reward is even more attractive than it was and so I have decided to upgrade the stock to a strong buy.

Demand Slightly Shaky

Accenture reported their FY2025 Q3 earnings on June 20th. They generated total revenues of $17.7 billion, up 7.66% YoY. This is quite a respectable growth rate as it is above the average of the past few years and is also an acceleration from the previous quarter. This figure also blew past expectations as they beat analyst estimates by $404.02 million. In their earnings release, you can see that the Americas remain as Accenture's largest geographic market by some margin and so it is understandable why investors had a negative reaction to potential DOGE spending cuts. As of now, revenue growth in the Americas is still a quite strong 8%.

Despite respectable revenue growth, bookings show that there may be weakness moving forward. For their Q3, they had a total of $19.7 billion in new bookings, a 6% decline from the prior year period. This is a worsening from a 3% decrease in their Q2 and so a trend of weaker demand may be occurring. Accenture did state that they had new generative AI bookings of $1.5 billion, however, which is an increase from the previous quarter's $1.4 billion. Therefore, despite some demand headwinds, their AI products do seem to be gaining traction. This is, of course, a very important signal to investors that the long term outlook is likely still a bright one for the company.

Margins Are Mixed

Accenture's Q2 was the worst quarter for gross margin in the past few years but do note that Q2 has historically been a weaker quarter for the company. Despite bouncing back strong for Q3, the gross margin figure of 32.86% is still a contraction from the year ago quarter's 33.4%. Looking at their income statement you can clearly see that the increase of cost of services was greater than revenue gains. While it's hard to tell from just these figures, Accenture likely either faced some pricing pressure, modest cost control issues, or maybe some of both. That said, the gross margin decline is not that severe, and so it's not a major concern as of now.

While cost of services was not managed particularly well, their operating expenses were nicely controlled. Operating margin was reported to be 16.8%, up 40 bps in adjusted terms or 80 bps in GAAP terms. Either way, corporate efficiency took a turn for the better. Sales and marketing, as well as general and administrative costs, both increased less than the pace of revenue growth, and thus they decreased as a percentage of revenues. Therefore, overall profitability of the business seemed strong and resilient.

Bottom Line Growth Rebounds

With the better efficiency, as discussed above, comes a strong bottom line. For Q3, Accenture reported a diluted EPS of $3.49, representing growth of 11.50% YoY. This is the highest growth rate in the past year and is a steep acceleration from Q2's 1.81% of growth. Therefore, despite all the worry, the company is experiencing robust profits. Looking ahead, you can see that analysts expect bottom line growth to moderate again in the next few quarters. However, even the weakest quarter, FY2026 Q1, is expected to have an EPS growth rate well above 3% and so I would say the company is likely to continue its run of resilience even though the macro-environment is unfavorable.

Juicy Capital Returns

In addition to a strong bottom line, Accenture saw respectable figures for their cash flows. For operating cash flows, they reported $3.684 billion, up 17.26% YoY. This actually outpaced EPS increases and so cash generation was impressive overall. When subtracting cash used for the purchases of property and equipment, you get FCFs of $3.515 billion. This figure is a 16.48% increase YoY and despite being far beneath the peak of FY2025 Q1, this is a decent growth rate. Also, note that Q3's FCF figure is the second highest of the past three years and so as a whole, I would consider Accenture's cash flows to be very healthy at this point.

In addition to strong cash flows, it seems that the company is becoming increasingly willing to share that cash with shareholders. In terms of dividends, in Q3, total payout was $924 million, a notable increase from $811 million in the prior year period. In per share terms, Accenture raised their dividend by 15% over the FY2024 rate to $1.48. As you can see above, Seeking Alpha Quant rates their dividend very favorably, and I agree that the dividend is a strong one even though the current yield of 2.41% isn't overly high. Don't forget that for Accenture, share buybacks are an even larger contributor to capital returns. For Q3, they bought back $1.7995 billion worth of stock. This is a massive 30% increase from the year ago quarter. While the increase in the dividend is nice, share buyback growth should be even more tempting to investors. With cash flows being healthy, I believe the company can afford to continue their generous capital returns to shareholders.

Full Year Guidance Mostly Raised

In my previous article, I stated that guidance for the full year showed strong resilience and confidence in their outlook. In their Q3 earnings release, Accenture raised their guidance for many key metrics. Probably the least favorable development for their outlook is the fact that they now expect operating margin to be 15.6% which is at the low end of their previously provided range. For revenue growth, they are now expecting 6-7% in local currency terms. Note that in FY2024, they saw just 2% growth in their local currency and so overall FY2025 is a strong year for the company's top line. For EPS, they expect 7-8% growth over FY2024's adjusted EPS (excludes business optimization costs). Note that adjusted EPS growth in the previous year was just 2% over FY2023 levels and so bottom line growth is also accelerating. Lastly, they are now expecting between $9.0 billion and $9.7 billion of FCF. That implies an 8.59% increase while in FY2024, they saw a 4.33% decrease. Therefore, cash flow strength is occurring on the annual time frame as well.

Valuation Even Cheaper Than Before

In the above chart, it is clear that the valuation of the company has gotten even cheaper since my previous article in April. The forward P/E ratio is currently very near multiyear lows and I believe its reading at 19.11 shows material undervaluation. As discussed earlier, an increase in operating margin has helped to power a major rebound in EPS growth for Q3. In the EPS chart, it is shown that some moderation in growth is expected but continued resilience is also expected. Furthermore, as stated above in the guidance section, the company raised their outlook for FY2025 for various metrics, including EPS. With the current fiscal year expected to be a notable improvement from FY2024 levels of growth, I would say the forward P/E ratio's massive contraction this year has been an irrational reaction. Despite all the noise of DOGE spending cuts, the company's fundamentals are rock-solid and its outlook is robust. Furthermore, note that the IT sector has a GAAP forward P/E of 30.56 as of this writing and so Accenture is at a 37.47% discount according to this measure. With the company being one of the key IT consulting companies in the world, I believe this large of a valuation gap creates considerable opportunity.

Takeaway

Accenture was a buy and now I believe it is a strong buy. In their Q3 results it is shown that top line growth reaccelerated. While there was some weakness in overall bookings, strong AI bookings show that the long term future is likely to be bright. For the near term, the company raised annual guidance for many metrics, suggesting that headwinds aren't having major effects on the company. While the fundamentals and outlook are getting increasingly strong, the valuation has gotten cheaper since my previous article. As stated in the introduction, this means that the risk/reward of the stock has gotten even more attractive. With that in mind, I believe my upgrade of Accenture to a strong buy rating is justified.

data.title: LyondellBasell Dividend: Reckless Decision Or Smart Capital Allocation?
data.description:
By now, it should come as no surprise to anyone that the chemical industry is undergoing one of the worst down cycles in history, and that LyondellBasell Industries (NYSE:LYB) is at its epicenter.

After quickly recovering from the 2020 volatility, LYB's share price has collapsed over the past 12 months and is now trading at multi-year lows (if we ignore the 2020 drop caused by the oil surplus during the pandemic).

It's important to keep in mind that the headwinds faced by LYB are industry-wide, and it's during such severe downturns when well-managed enterprises are separated from the rest.

The first part from the statement above is evident from the identical share price performance between LYB and Dow Inc. (NYSE:DOW). Proving the second part of the statement would be much harder to do and only time will tell whether decisions made by LYB's management were the right ones.

The good news is that we're already seeing some signs that the trough of the current cycle is not very far off. The graph above is already showing some divergence in share price performance between LYB and DOW, an early sign that one of them is doing something differently.

One of the key differences between LYB and DOW is their approach to capital allocation, and more specifically, the dividend. Just very recently, DOW's management has done what is considered to be the prudent thing during a market downturn, and that's to cut the dividend.

LYB management, on the other hand, has done the opposite and raised the dividend earlier this year.

Such a divergence between the two largest players is quite unusual and is a signal that LYB's management decisions are either far superior to those of DOW's management or simply that they are being reckless.

Following these developments, LYB's dividend yield is now above 10%, which is often seen as a red flag before an eventual dividend cut. But if that doesn't happen, anyone who bought LYB recently could be amply rewarded.

Down below, I take a closer look at whether it's worth taking this bet after most investors have already thrown in the towel.

The Cash Flow Perspective

The gap between LYB's annual dividend payments and the cash flow from operations has almost disappeared in recent months. This wasn't a big issue just six months ago, but the graph is no longer encouraging for anyone in the "no dividend cut" camp.

Once we include the trailing 12 months' worth of capital expenditures of almost $1.9 billion, we see that LYB's free cash flow for the period is now barely in positive territory. There were, however, a number of things that allowed LYB's management to retain its dividends and that set the company apart from DOW.

The first thing was the company's significantly larger (on a relative basis) cash holdings. As we see on the graph below, in the critical 2023 and 2024 periods, LYB's cash at hand and short-term investments as a share of total assets were significantly higher than those of DOW. Having said that, however, the two companies are now in a similar position as far as cash & cash equivalents are concerned.

Another notable observation is that LYB has reinvested significantly larger amounts (again on a relative basis) back into its business in recent years. This is clearly illustrated by LYB's significantly higher capex to depreciation & amortization expense in recent years (particularly before 2023).

This now allows the company to reduce its capital expenditure going forward, without this resulting in significant disruption of ongoing operations.

We continue to believe that the combination of our proprietary MoReTec technology and our existing infrastructure will provide LYB with a material competitive advantage as we establish ourselves as a profitable leader in the circular economy. But given uncertainty in the timing of the market recovery, we will postpone this investment decision until market conditions improve.

Source: LYB Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript

During the last quarter, the management has outlined the current planned capex reductions and they'll be significant in FY 2026. For 2025, the company has so far spent $1 billion on capex, which leaves $700 million for the second half of the year.

Cash flow from operations has been quite low for the first half of 2025, mostly on the back of changes in working capital in addition to the record-low profitability. What we note, however, is that in the last quarter of each fiscal year, we have a massive increase in cash flow from operations due to lower receivables, and this effect usually reverses in the first quarter of the following year. With this dynamic in mind, the third quarter of this year will be crucial when determining the safety of LYB's dividend.

The cash at hand right now also stands at around $1.7 billion, good news given the quarterly dividend outlay of around $450 million. Moreover, LYB's management recently announced the sale of four large assets in Europe, which will provide some cash inflow in the coming quarters.

We continue to make progress on our strategic objectives, and we're pleased to announce the proposed sale of our 4 European O&P assets in June.

Source: LYB Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript

Profitability within the Olefins and Polyolefins International has been quite low, which means that the sale of these assets would not have a material impact on LYB's profitability.

Also, there will be no more share buybacks through the rest of 2025 and in the following year.

We're also not planning any further share buybacks over 2025 and 2026.

Source: LYB Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript

With all that in mind, in the absence of any negative industry or market-wide developments, it seems highly likely that LYB would be able to sustain its dividend through the rest of 2025.

In 2026, we'll have quarterly capex requirements of around $350 million and dividend outlays of roughly $450 million, which combined would require cash flow from operations to be at least $800 million on a quarterly basis. Looking back, this seems achievable; however, benchmark prices of various end-products remain low, and they have continued to decline in the first half of 2025.

The key risk for LYB's dividend remains economic activity in 2026, and mostly demand of ethylene and polyethylene as market supply remains high.

Conclusion

Contrary to its major peer, LyondellBasell seems to be in a better position to weather the current storm in the sector. The decision to increase the quarterly dividend still seems like an unnecessary risk, but the dividend so far appears safe as long as there are no significant shocks to the economy. Beyond 2025, things are less certain, albeit the watermark for quarterly cash flow from operations that would need to be achieved to cover the dividend and capex is not very high. Based on all that, I remain optimistic that the 10% dividend yield is worth the risk as long as one's equity portfolio is properly diversified.

data.title: HYG: Everything You Need To Know About The High Yield Bond ETF
data.description:
The evolution of financial engineering and the trajectory of retail investors' cross-asset activity provides latitude to index fund creators, especially those involved in the fixed-income sphere. Moreover, peripheral opportunities exist for analysts, whereby unbundling the key aspects of index funds and communicating the findings to audiences, introduces value through informational symmetry. Today's article aims to achieve exactly that – by covering the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:HYG), we attempted to delineate its ebbs, flows, and nuances – consolidated into one report. Moreover, we decided to maintain a neutral opinion of HYG, leaving the floor open for readers to draw their own conclusions.

Without further delay, herewith is an explanation of the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF and its salient aspects.

iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF's Strategy

Headline Approach

HYG tracks the Markit iBoxx® USD Liquid High Yield Index by investing at least 80% of its total assets in the index. Moreover, the ETF uses securities lending and derivatives contracts to keep its portfolio's exposure near the index's – HYG deploys the strategy to stay nimble, it can be challenging shifting weights of underlying securities.

A closer look at HYG's underlying index's composition conveys a few salient talking points. Firstly, the index has an adjusted market-cap weighted and North America geared infrastructure, limiting its issuer exposure to ~3%. In addition, the index only selects high-yielding bonds with "an average rating of sub-investment grade," maturities between six-months and 15-years, and outstanding face values of at least, $400 million, depending on the ratings agency used. Furthermore, the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF's underlying index diversifies company-specific risk, shown by the fact that its issuer-based exposure exceeded 1,200 in February 2025. To cap it off, the index includes "Fixed coupon bonds (“plain vanilla bonds”), step-up bonds with coupon schedules known at issuance (or as functions of the issuer’s rating), bonds with sinking funds, medium term notes."

What about HYG's returns? A ten-year look-back signals cyclical performance. The ETF has delivered 4.05% in pre-tax annualized returns via a blend of cyclical price and income-based returns. Unlike some fixed-income ETFs, HYG distributes its income and doesn't distribute capital gains nor return of capital.

A Heuristic Explanation

To simplify the previous section, I'd say: HYG invests in an index, which tracks non-investment grade corporate credit bonds, otherwise known as junk bonds. The vehicle's mandate is quantified by observing its option-adjusted spread (a measure of option-adjusted credit spread) of ~269 basis points, which is more-or-less in-line with the ICE BofA US High Yield Index Spread. Despite being credit risk-seeking, HYG assumes minimal interest rate price risk, shown by its effective duration of 2.84 years.

Combining the ETF's systematic traits leads to a current weighted average coupon of around 6.49%, a 30-day SEC yield of 6.47%, three-year standard deviation of 6.46%, and a weighted average maturity of 3.53 years. Interestingly, the ETF has negative convexity, conveying a degree of non-linearity in its return structure – while a myriad of reasons could explain the convexity figure, optionality remains the most likely reason.

The previous section illustrated the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF's annualized returns. Herewith is a heuristic view of the ETF's past returns, illustrating its reliance on distributions rather than price returns. However, as mentioned later in the article, HYG occasionally suffers from tail risk, which underscores the need to monitor the credit cycle and its potential influence on HYG's point-in-time market values.

Influencing Variables

HYG's Credit Risk

Credit risk measured via the US High Yield B Option-Adjusted Spread shows a negative correlation with HYG's price returns, suggesting a spike in credit risk can lead to HYG shedding market value. A spike in spreads usually occurs when bond investors believe the economy is bound to enter into a recession and/or expectations of higher default probabilities emerge – for more on OAS, kindly visit this link.

Similarly, the OAS influences HYG's total returns, suggesting its income-based returns rarely mitigate a spike in credit spreads. Given its broad issuer diversification, higher OAS probably won't influence dividend passthroughs. However, HYG has a maximum drawdown, since inception, of 34.28%, meaning a market-based event can override HYG's distribution yield.

So, how do we treat OAS?

Simplistically, OAS provides an expected return premium. Widening of the premium usually adds to loan-level coupon pricing and detracts from market values, providing evidence that investing in higher spreads would usually result in enhanced returns while investing in lower spreads can deplete future returns. Mechanically, OAS provides an expected return instead of a guaranteed return, meaning, analysis of the risks involved in the spread's risks is salient – its essentially a question of: will the risk premium provided be harvested or is the market still underpricing risk?

Side Note: Coupon levels on fixed-rate bonds won't change alongside OAS, unless met with contingent terms or restructuring. However, HYG recycles its holdings to maintain a targeted duration. As such, newly issued fixed-rate bonds will likely reflect OAS' influence on HYG's weighted average coupon metric, upon and after their acquisition.

The bulk of what I mentioned in the previous paragraph refers to a generalized high-yield portfolio. A deeper dive would require assessment of HYG's ratings-based exposure and how sensitive each rating is to changes in OAS. As matters stand, the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF possesses primary exposure to BB-B ratings. The make-up might change as time passes. However, don't expect rapid changes in ratings-based exposure unless mass credit migration occurs.

Another means of assessing HYG's market-based credit risk (still referring to OAS) would be to dial in on its sector-based exposure. Cyclical sectors have higher credit spread volatility, whereas counter-cyclical sectors own less OAS volatility. In our view, key metrics to look at would be sector interest coverage ratios, forward earnings growth rates, asset/liability spreads, and OAS cycles – herewith is a view of HYG's current sectoral exposure, as with its ratings-based exposure, HYG's sectoral exposure isn't static.

Interest Rates

Interest rates provide an additional influencing variable. Theoretically, it would be productive observing treasury yields instead of realized rates, given a yield curve's forward-looking nature. However, for explanatory purposes, I found the Secured Overnight Funding Rate to work just fine as an explanatory variable.

As illustrated in the following diagram, HYG's market value has a negative relationship with SOFR. As an add-on, effective duration measures a bond ETF's price sensitivity to a parallel shift in interest rates – despite possessing short-to-intermediate duration, HYG has shown noteworthy sensitivity to rates in the past.

The iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF's total returns are less influenced by rates than its isolated price returns, illustrating effective cushioning from its income-based component. Nonetheless, sporadic dislocations have occurred, most likely due to lagged responses in coupons (on new acquisitions), inflation, and discount rates.

Where does all this leave the analysis?

Firstly, we need to distinguish between fixed-rate and floating-rate securities. Unlike the OAS' impact, interest rates don't have a homogenous influence on fixed and floating-rate securities. Existing fixed rate securities will likely experience devaluation as interest rates (yields) move up due to their coupons not adjusting alongside discount rates. On the other end, newly-issued fixed-rate securities and existing floating-rate securities will see higher coupons during the rate hiking process. Moreover, floating-rate coupons usually aren't systematically devalued when rates rise because their coupons rise in tandem.

From the above and HYG's key exposure (referenced in sub-section 1), we can gather the following: HYG will likely experience devaluation when rates rise but present a lucrative total return opportunity once rates have peaked – that's mechanically speaking and keeping other factors constant.

Credit Migration (Jump-to-Default)

Credit migration refers to issuer rating changes. In our experience, OAS predicts credit risk much better than ratings changes do. However, ratings changes can influence the underlying holdings' values, especially because credit risk 'jumps-to-default' and rarely follows a linear deterioration or improvement. In addition, we often recognize such jumps when leading credit ratings agencies such as Moody's (MCO), S&P Global (SPGI), or Fitch release hard data.

Risk-Return Attribution

I mentioned earlier that iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF has substantial drawdown risk. Here's a juxtaposition, illustrating the vehicle's max drawdowns for the past ten years and since inception.

Max drawdown is an isolated metric. Therefore, I decided to draw-up a few others, paying close attention to both risk and return. Herewith is a summation of each; a discussion follows the diagram.

From the above, we can gather the following.

  • The Sharpe Ratio measures volatility adjusted excess returns. It provides an idea of whether an asset's total risk is justified by excess returns. HYG's Sharpe Ratio is in positive territory and tracks near BND ETF (used as a benchmark, illustrating positive risk-adjusted returns.
  • The Sortino Ratio functions similar to the Sharpe Ratio. However, instead of measuring total risk and using the risk-free rate as a return benchmark, the Sortino ratio uses downside deviation and a discretionary benchmark. HYG's results dispense similar results to the Sharpe Ratio.
  • Calmar measures annualized returns versus max drawdown. The ratio conveys worst-case risk assumed in pursuit of returns. HYG's results convey a positive relationship, whereby its drawdown risk has been warranted by its annualized returns.
  • A monthly 5% Value-at-Risk of 3.73% essentially means HYG suffered a maximum monthly loss of 3.73% for 95% of its tailing months. The metrics parts between worst-case scenarios and tail risk – the relationship between HYG's max drawdown and VaR shows disparity.

Portfolio Suitability

Broadly Diversified Portfolios

The iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF can be a yield enhancer for broadly diversified portfolios. As a higher beta, high-yielding asset, the ETF might provide excess risk-adjusted returns to well-balanced portfolios, assuming carefully planned correlation risk structures and tax trade-offs.

Rotational Portfolios

In light of its cyclicality, one could argue that HYG suits rotational portfolios, seeking excess return. It's a pro-cyclical asset, making it an ideal candidate for market timing portfolios.

Leveraged Portfolios With Tail Risk Hedging

Higher-risk leveraged portfolios may benefit from HYG under prerequisites such as affordable financing rates and tail risk management abilities. That being said, complacency, unfavorable funding rates, and illiquidity can dampen this high-risk strategy.

Conclusion

The iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF tracks the Markit iBoxx® USD Liquid High Yield Index. Methodologically, the index provides issuer-based diversification but maintains noteworthy credit spread risk exposure. The vehicle's short-to-intermediate term duration limits interest rates' influence on pricing; however, coupon entry rates may differ significantly depending on rate regimes.

A study of the ETF's key risk-return metrics illustrates telling worst-case risks. However, in contrast, salient risk-based pricing metrics convey warranted risk-return attribution. With that, the asset likely suits well-balanced portfolios seeking a yield kicker, rotational portfolios, and tail risk-managed leveraged portfolios.

In essence, HYG is a cyclical asset and demands close attention to credit regime shifts.

This article answers these three main questions about HYG:

  • What type of investor is HYG best suited for?
  • Are HYG's returns consistent or is there cyclacality to them?
  • Would HYG be considered a high-risk, high-reward ETF?
